title: Insuring an off-grid or non-standard construction property
subtitle: How to present your property to specialist insurers, what exclusions to watch for, and which systems need specific cover
excerpt: Standard household insurance policies are written for properties with mains services and conventional construction. An off-grid property (particularly one with alternative water supply, solid fuel heating, solar systems, or unconventional materials) requires specialist assessment to ensure cover is not voided by an undisclosed material fact. This is the disclosure and comparison guide for getting adequate cover.
sourceNotes: Association of British Insurers 'Non-standard construction' guidance. Financial Conduct Authority ICOBS 6A disclosure obligations. Which? home insurance non-standard construction guide. MMA Insurance and Towergate specialist rural property underwriting criteria.

glossaryTerms.definition: Information that would influence a reasonable insurer's decision to offer cover or the terms on which it is offered. Under UK insurance law, policyholders have a duty to disclose all material facts. An undisclosed material fact (such as a non-mains water supply or alternative heating system) can render a policy void at the point of claim.

Any building that does not have brick or stone walls and a tiled or slated pitched roof. Includes timber frame, cob, straw bale, earth sheltered, converted agricultural buildings, and properties with flat roofs or non-standard wall materials. Non-standard construction requires disclosure to insurers and usually specialist underwriting.

content.text: A standard home insurance policy's underwriting assumptions (mains water, mains drainage, gas or oil central heating, conventional construction) are all
material facts
. If your property departs from these, that departure must be disclosed. The risk of non-disclosure is not a premium uplift. It is a policy voided at the point when you need to claim.
What to disclose
Construction materials:
non-standard construction
must be specified. List cob, straw bale, timber frame, converted outbuildings, flat roof proportion, and any rooms added with non-matching materials.
Water supply: borehole, spring, rainwater harvesting, or surface water abstraction. List the treatment system (UV, filtration) and the date of the last water quality test.
Drainage and sewage: septic tank, reed bed, composting toilet, or biodigester. Include the last emptying or service record date.
Heating: any solid fuel stove, biomass boiler, wood gasifier, or uncertified heating system. A HETAS certificate for solid fuel is valuable evidence.

Electrical systems: off-grid solar PV, battery storage, wind turbine, or micro-hydro. List the installation date and whether it was professionally fitted. Add a DNO notification or MCS certificate number where you have one.
Finding specialist insurers

Standard comparison sites will either decline the property or return quotes that are voided on the policy detail. Use specialist rural insurers. When you call, have a fact sheet ready: construction type, wall and roof materials, utility services, heating, and renewable systems. Giving full details upfront prevents the most common denial: failing to disclose a material fact.
Common exclusions to check

Escape of water from private supply systems: many policies only cover escape of water from a mains-connected system. Check whether your policy covers burst pipes or tank overflow on a private water supply.

Fire damage from solid fuel appliances: some policies require a HETAS-certified installation and annual service record. Without these, a chimney fire claim may be refused on the grounds of poor maintenance.
Solar PV and battery storage: inverters and batteries may not be covered under standard policies. Ask if installed solar kit is covered at full replacement cost.
Unoccupied property clauses: many policies cut cover after 30 or 60 consecutive days empty. Second homes and holiday lets need specific empty-property wording.
Rebuilding cost vs market value

Insurance is written on rebuilding cost, not market value. Non-standard materials (cob, straw bale, oak frame) cost more per square metre to rebuild than conventional brick because of specialist labour. Under-insuring means any claim payout is proportionally reduced. A RICS rebuilding cost assessment gives the correct figure; this costs 200 to 500 pounds and is worth doing before insuring any non-standard property.
